title: Access Specifiers
description: public, private, and protected: controlling what's reachable from outside a class, and the getter/setter pattern this enables.
objectives: Understand public, private, and protected access levels
See what happens when private members are accessed from outside the class
Use the getter/setter pattern to control access to private data
Know the default access level differs between class and struct

sections.text: 1. Recap: public: in Classes and Objects
The Student class used public: without explaining it. public is one of three access specifiers that control what code outside the class is allowed to touch.

rows: Yes — freely accessible from anywhere the object is visible
No — only accessible from inside the class's own member functions
No from outside, but yes from a derived class (a preview — covered fully in Inheritance)

sections.text: acc.balance is invalid the moment balance is marked private — code outside BankAccount simply cannot reach it, no exceptions. This is a compile-time error, not a runtime warning.

This is a first taste of Encapsulation, its own topic soon. Making balance private prevents code elsewhere in the program from setting it to something nonsensical, like a negative balance, without going through logic the class itself controls.

sections.title: What this buys us
points: balance itself stays private — nothing outside the class can touch it directly
setBalance is a public member function — a controlled gateway that can validate before changing balance
getBalance is a public member function — a controlled way to read balance without exposing it directly
This pair is called getters and setters — a very common OOP pattern for controlled access

sections.text: This is a genuinely common interview question: class and struct in C++ are nearly identical, but their default access level differs. Members are private by default in a class, and public by default in a struct, if no access specifier is written at all.

sections.title: A bank vault vs the lobby
sections.text: A bank's lobby (public) is open to any visitor. The vault (private) is only accessible to bank staff working from behind the counter — a customer can't just walk in and grab cash directly, they have to go through a teller (a public member function) who follows the bank's rules before touching the vault's contents.

sections.title: Common Beginner Mistakes
items: Trying to access a private member directly from outside the class — this is a compile-time error, not something caught only at runtime.
Forgetting class members default to private, and being confused why direct access fails with no access specifier written.
Making everything public out of convenience — defeats the purpose of controlling how a class's data can be changed.
Writing a setter with no validation at all — the entire benefit of a setter is the chance to check the incoming value before accepting it.

sections.title: Interview Questions

questions.question: What are the three access specifiers in C++?
questions.answer: public, private, and protected, controlling whether a member is accessible from outside the class, only within the class, or accessible to derived classes as well.

questions.question: What happens if you try to access a private member from outside its class?
questions.answer: It's a compile-time error — private members are only reachable from the class's own member functions.

questions.question: What is the default access level for members of a class versus a struct?
questions.answer: private by default in a class, public by default in a struct, when no access specifier is explicitly written.

questions.question: What is the getter/setter pattern, and why is it useful?
questions.answer: Public member functions (get*/set*) that provide controlled access to a private member, letting a setter validate incoming values instead of allowing direct, unchecked modification.

sections.thirtySecondAnswer: Access specifiers control what's reachable from outside a class: public members are freely accessible, private members are only accessible from the class's own member functions, and protected members add accessibility for derived classes, covered fully in Inheritance. A class's members default to private, while a struct's default to public. The getter/setter pattern uses this: a member variable is kept private, and public functions provide controlled read and write access — letting a setter validate a value before it's ever assigned, which is the foundation of Encapsulation.

sections.title: Trick to Remember Access Specifiers
sections.text: public = the lobby, anyone welcome. private = the vault, staff only. Getter/setter = the teller who checks the rules before touching the vault for you.
